nativeRange.habitat: The Tench is found in still and slow-moving freshwater, including lakes, ponds, lowland rivers, backwaters, and oxbow lakes. It prefers clay or muddy substrates with dense aquatic vegetation and tolerates low dissolved oxygen levels, occasionally occupying brackish margins.

decorPreferences: soft sandy or muddy substrate for digging and rooting
dense submersed vegetation (hornwort, water milfoil, pondweed)
submerged roots or large smooth rocks as shelter
dim or shaded zones — crepuscular species that avoids bright light

diet.notes: The Tench is a bottom-feeding omnivore with a preference for animal matter. It feeds mainly at dusk and into the night on aquatic insect larvae — including chironomid larvae, commonly sold as bloodworm — along with small snails, pea clams, worms, and small crustaceans. It also grazes on algae and soft plant material. In pond culture, tench readily accept sinking pellets, pond sticks, live or frozen bloodworm, daphnia, and chopped earthworms. Feeding activity slows dramatically below 8°C and ceases entirely near 4°C; fish become torpid during cold overwinter periods and do not require feeding.

avoidWith: small fish (fry predation risk with very large adults)

imageCandidates.notes: Karelj; 2400x1618. Underwater shot of two live tench in a natural setting — multiple individuals suit this shoaling species; shows the diagnostic deep olive-bronze body with golden iridescent flanks, small red eye and dark rounded fins.

Dezidor; 2816x2112. Sharp underwater close-up with vibrant golden-green iridescence, copper-red eye and clearly visible mouth barbel — best diagnostic colour; caveat: tight framing crops the caudal fin, so kept as non-recommended.

Bernard DUPONT; 3433x2559. Crisp complete lateral view of a live specimen showing full body proportions, thick stocky build, rounded fins, red eye and olive-to-cream flank gradient; out-of-water grass background.

summary: The Tench is a large cool-water cyprinid native to Europe and western Asia, known in the pond hobby for its hardiness and peaceful temperament. Growing to 30–50 cm and living 15–25 years, it needs a permanent outdoor pond at adult size. Its folkloric name, Doctor Fish, stems from an old belief that sick fish would rub against its thick mucus coat for healing — a claim without scientific basis.
careNotes: Tench are hardy, undemanding pond fish that thrive in cool, still or slow-moving freshwater with soft substrate and dense planting. They are crepuscular and nocturnal bottom feeders that disturb sediment while rooting for invertebrates — this behaviour is a natural part of their ecology but can increase turbidity in small, lightly planted ponds. A soft sandy or clay substrate of at least 10 cm depth is strongly recommended so fish can root comfortably without abrading their barbels. Tench tolerate a broad water chemistry range (pH 6.5–8.0, gH 5–25) and are one of the most hypoxia-tolerant European freshwater fish, surviving in conditions that would stress carp. Despite this resilience, good filtration and periodic water changes remain important in smaller garden ponds. Tench grow large — commonly 30–50 cm in outdoor ponds — and should never be kept in indoor aquaria at adult size. Juvenile tench (under about 15 cm) can be started in very large indoor tanks briefly, but pond transfer should be planned early. Winter care: tench become torpid below about 8°C and effectively hibernate at the pond bottom under ice; cease feeding below 8°C to avoid uneaten food fouling water. A cold, dark overwintering period (4–8°C) is actually a prerequisite for inducing natural breeding behaviour. The 'Doctor Fish' name derives from old European folklore claiming that other ailing fish would rub against the tench’s famously thick, slippery mucus coat to be healed — even that pike and perch spared tench out of gratitude. This is biologically unfounded, though the slime coat does make tench themselves exceptionally disease-resistant in practice. Tench are listed as a regulated or prohibited invasive species in several US states (including Washington, Michigan, and Connecticut); check local regulations before acquiring.
breedingNotes: Tench spawn in late spring to summer (May–August) when water temperature reaches 19–20°C, among dense submersed vegetation in shallow water. Females are notably larger than males and can be distinguished by broader pelvic fins in mature adults. A single female produces 300,000–600,000 small, adhesive pale-green eggs that adhere to aquatic plants; no parental care is provided after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–6 days at 20°C. Larvae initially cling to plants before becoming free-swimming. Sexual maturity is reached at 2–3 years for males and 3–7 years for females. Successful captive breeding in indoor aquaria has not been documented; a cold overwintering period (4–8°C) followed by a natural spring warming cycle appears to be required to trigger spawning. Pond breeding with natural seasonal cues is achievable but fry survival requires very dense vegetation for cover.

primary.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Tench (Tinca tinca) species profile
primary.notes: Primary trade/care source. Provided temperature (4–24°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (dH up to 25), adult size (60 cm+), diet (bottom feeder, sinking foods), compatibility (peaceful, unaggressive), native range (Europe and Asia), breeding (May–August, adhesive eggs, 6–8 day hatch), and the Doctor Fish folklore context.

additional.name: FishBase — Tinca tinca
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cypriniformes; family listed as Tincidae on FishBase but Cyprinidae per user brief and most hobby sources — retained Cyprinidae per task constraint), maximum size (70 cm SL), temperature range (4–24°C), native range (Eurasia to Yenisei drainage), diet (detritus, benthic animals, plant materials, molluscs), spawning temperature (19–20°C), generation time (~8.9 yr), habitat (shallow densely vegetated freshwater/brackish).

additional.name: Aqualog — The Tench: Facts and Fables
additional.notes: Specialist aquarium magazine article. Provided aquarium-specific notes: optimal temperature 12–26°C, cold overwintering (4–8°C) as breeding prerequisite, aquarium size context (15–20 cm attained in tanks vs 40 cm in ponds), substrate requirements (soft sandy), dim lighting preference (crepuscular), diet (insect larvae, small crustaceans, snails, bivalves), the Doctor Fish folklore detail including the pike/perch legend, and note that captive breeding is theoretically possible but unreported.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Tench
additional.notes: Used for native range confirmation (Western Europe and British Isles east to Ob/Yenisei rivers; Lake Baikal), habitat (still waters, clay/muddy substrate, dense vegetation), low-oxygen tolerance, diet (chironomids, snails, pea clams), Doctor Fish folklore etymology, and invasive establishment context in North America.

additional.name: USGS Nonindigenous Aquatic Species — Tinca tinca
additional.notes: Confirmed invasive establishment in 38 US states; documents introductions from California (1922) through Mississippi River watershed and Great Lakes basin; supports hobbyistAdvisory invasive regulatory note.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: European pond fish reaching 50cm and pond-only at maturity. Listed as invasive in many US states (regulated).

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding requires a cold overwintering period of 4–8°C followed by a natural spring warming cycle to trigger spawning. Tench spawn from May through August among dense submersed vegetation once water temperatures reach 19–20°C. Females can produce 300,000 to 600,000 small, adhesive eggs with no parental care after spawning. Males typically reach sexual maturity at 2–3 years, while females mature later at 3–7 years. Captive indoor breeding has not been documented; a pond setting with natural seasonal cues appears to be required.
